Node 54 — Lawful Purpose
Definition: A contract must not be made for illegal purposes or against public interest; insurance contracts must have lawful purposes.
Category: Valid Contract Req.
Tags: Legal, Insurance

Node 55 — Insurable Interest
Definition: The requirement that a policyowner or beneficiary has a genuine financial or emotional interest in the insured’s risk; prevents wagering on lives.
Category: Valid Contract Req.
Tags: Insurance, Legal
Related Nodes: CV01, CR01, CR02, CR05, IR01, IR02, IR03, IR04

text:
Node 56 — Contractual Capacity
Definition: The ability of parties to enter into a valid contract; insurance companies must be authorized; individuals must not be minors or mentally incapacitated.
Category: Valid Contract Req.
Tags: Insurance, Legal
Related Nodes: CV01, CR01, IR05, IR06

Definition: Insurable interest must exist at policy issuance, not necessarily at the time of claim or death.

Node 61 — Contractual Capacity - Minors
Definition: Minors lack full contractual capacity; insurance contracts with minors are voidable by the minor.

Tags: Insurance, Legal

Node 62 — Contractual Capacity - Mental
Definition: Individuals declared mentally incompetent lack contractual capacity; contracts with such persons are void or voidable.
Category: Insurance Requirement
Tags: Insurance, Legal

text:
Node 63 — Actuary
Definition: A professional who specializes in calculating the financial impact of risk and uncertainty. They use the mathematics of insurance to determine premium rates so the company can pay future benefits, cover expenses, and make a profit.
Category: Role
Tags: Insurance, Actuarial Science, Risk Management, Pricing
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-005, KN-006, KN-007, KN-009, KN-012

text:
Node 64 — Premium Rate
Definition: A rate established by actuaries per unit of insurance coverage (typically per $1,000 per year). It is used to calculate the annual premium amount for a policy.
Category: Concept
Tags: Insurance, Pricing, Actuarial Science
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-003, KN-004, KN-012

text:
Node 65 — Premium Amount
Definition: The total amount a policyowner pays for an insurance policy for a given period. It is calculated by multiplying the premium rate by the number of coverage units.
Category: Concept
Tags: Insurance, Pricing
Related Nodes: KN-002

text:
Node 66 — Pricing Factors
Definition: The key elements that actuaries account for when developing premium rates: cost of benefits, investment earnings, and operating expenses, along with a margin for profit.
Category: Concept
Tags: Insurance, Pricing, Actuarial Science
Related Nodes: KN-005, KN-006, KN-007, KN-008

text:
Node 67 — Cost of Benefits
Definition: All of the insurer’s potential benefit payments (like death or surrender benefits) multiplied by the expected probability that each benefit will be payable.
Category: Factor
Tags: Insurance, Pricing, Actuarial Science
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-009, KN-011

text:
Node 68 — Investment Earnings
Definition: The income generated by an insurer from investing a portion of the premiums it collects. These earnings allow insurers to charge lower premium rates.
Category: Factor
Tags: Insurance, Pricing, Finance
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-013, KN-014

text:
Node 69 — Operating Expenses
Definition: The costs associated with running the insurance company, including taxes, salaries, commissions, and office maintenance, which must be covered by the premium.
Category: Factor
Tags: Insurance, Pricing, Business Operations
Related Nodes: KN-004

Node 70 — Profit
Definition: The amount of money an insurer aims to make after all benefits and expenses are paid. It is a crucial component of pricing to ensure the company's financial strength and stability.
Category: Factor
Tags: Insurance, Pricing, Business Operations
Related Nodes: KN-004

Node 71 — Mortality Rate
Definition: The incidence of death, by age, among a given group of people. It is a key predictor used to determine the cost of benefits for life insurance.
Category: Metric
Tags: Actuarial Science, Demographics, Pricing
Related Nodes: KN-005, KN-010, KN-012

Node 72 — Mortality Table
Definition: A chart that displays the mortality rates for a given group of people at each age, used by actuaries for pricing life insurance products.
Category: Tool
Tags: Actuarial Science, Pricing

Node 73 — Lapse Rate
Definition: The annual percentage of policies that are terminated because the policyowner stops paying premiums. This is projected by actuaries to account for policy terminations.

Tags: Actuarial Science, Pricing

Node 74 — Block of Policies
Definition: A group of policies issued to insureds who share the same characteristics, such as age, sex, and risk classification. Insurers apply the same premium rate to all policies in a block.

text:
Node 77 — Policy Reserves
Definition: Liabilities on an insurer's balance sheet representing the estimated amount needed to pay future policy benefits. State laws require insurers to establish and maintain these reserves.
Category: Concept
Tags: Insurance, Regulation, Finance
Related Nodes: KN-006

text:
Node 78 — Risk Class
Definition: A category assigned to a proposed insured by an underwriter based on their level of risk. Common classes are Standard, Preferred, and Substandard, which directly affect the premium rate.
Category: Concept
Tags: Insurance, Underwriting
Related Nodes: KN-012

text:
Node 79 — Value Exchange
Definition: The principle that parties in a valid contract must exchange something of value. In insurance, the policyowner pays premiums in exchange for the insurer accepting the risk of financial loss.
Category: Principle
Tags: Insurance, Contract Law
Related Nodes:

text:
Node 80 — Term Life Insurance
Definition: Life insurance that provides a death benefit only if the insured dies during the period specified in the policy. It offers temporary financial protection for a set term.
Category: Product Type
Tags: life insurance, death benefit, temporary coverage, term, tử kỳ
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-003, KN-004, KN-008, KN-009, KN-010

text:
Node 81 — Level Term Insurance
Definition: A type of term life insurance where the death benefit and usually the premium remain the same throughout the policy term.
Category: Product Sub-Type
Tags: term life, fixed benefit, level premium, tử kỳ cố định
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 82 — Decreasing Term Insurance
Definition: A type of term life insurance where the death benefit decreases at stated intervals over the term of coverage, while the premium usually remains the same.
Category: Product Sub-Type
Tags: term life, declining benefit, mortgage, debt, tử kỳ giảm dần

text:
Node 83 — Increasing Term Insurance
Definition: A type of term life insurance where the death benefit increases at stated intervals over the term of coverage, often to account for inflation or growing financial needs.
Category: Product Sub-Type
Tags: term life, growing benefit, inflation protection, tử kỳ tăng dần

text:
Node 84 — Mortgage Insurance
Definition: A common form of decreasing term insurance designed to provide a benefit amount that corresponds to the decreasing balance owed on a mortgage loan.
Category: Product Example
Tags: decreasing term, debt protection, mortgage loan, home ownership

text:
Node 85 — Credit Life Insurance
Definition: A type of decreasing term insurance that pays the outstanding balance on short-term loans (e.g., auto, personal). The benefit is always payable to the lender/creditor.
Category: Product Example
Tags: decreasing term, loan protection, debt, creditor
Related Nodes: KN-003

text:
Node 86 — Family Income Coverage
Definition: A type of decreasing term insurance that pays a stated monthly income benefit to the beneficiary if the insured dies during the coverage term, continuing until the end of the specified term.
Category: Product Example
Tags: decreasing term, income replacement, monthly benefit, family support
Related Nodes: KN-003

text:
Node 87 — Return of Premium (ROP) Term Insurance
Definition: A type of term insurance that pays a death benefit if the insured dies during the term, but returns all premiums paid if the insured outlives the policy term. Premiums are higher than traditional term.
Category: Product Feature
Tags: term life, premium refund, ROP, bảo hiểm tử kỳ hoàn phí
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 88 — Renewable Term Life Insurance
Definition: A feature that allows a policyowner to continue term coverage for another policy term without providing new evidence of insurability. The premium increases based on the insured's attained age at renewal.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: renewal, continuation, evidence of insurability, attained age, gia hạn
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-011, KN-012, KN-014

text:
Node 89 — Convertible Term Life Insurance
Definition: A feature that gives the policyowner the right to change (convert) the term policy to a cash value (permanent) life insurance policy without providing evidence of insurability.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: conversion, permanent life, cash value, chuyển đổi
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-011, KN-012, KN-013

text:
Node 90 — Evidence of Insurability
Definition: Proof that an individual continues to be an insurable risk, typically involving medical exams or health questionnaires. It is not required for renewal or conversion features.
Category: Concept
Tags: underwriting, risk assessment, insurability, health status
Related Nodes: KN-009, KN-010

text:
Node 91 — Attained Age Conversion
Definition: A method of converting a term policy to a permanent policy where the premium for the new policy is based on the insured's age at the time of conversion.
Category: Policy Action
Tags: conversion, premium calculation, attained age
Related Nodes: KN-010, KN-013

text:
Node 92 — Original Age Conversion
Definition: A method of converting a term policy to a permanent policy where the premium for the new policy is based on the insured's age when the original term policy was issued.
Category: Policy Action
Tags: conversion, premium calculation, original age
Related Nodes: KN-010, KN-012

text:
Node 93 — Antiselection
Definition: The tendency of individuals with a higher-than-average risk of loss to seek or continue insurance coverage to a greater extent than those with an average or below-average risk. Insurers charge more for renewable/convertible policies to account for this.
Category: Concept
Tags: adverse selection, risk, insurance pricing
Related Nodes: KN-009, KN-010

text:
Node 94 — Cash Value Life Insurance
Definition: A type of life insurance that provides coverage for the insured's entire lifetime and includes a savings element known as the cash value. Also known as permanent life insurance.
Category: Product Category
Tags: Life Insurance, Permanent Insurance, Cash Value
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-003, KN-004, KN-005, KN-006

payload.tags: Life Insurance, Permanent Insurance, Cash Value

text:
Node 95 — Lifetime Coverage
Definition: A feature of cash value life insurance that protects the insured for their entire life, as long as the policy remains in force. The insured cannot outlive the protection.
Category: Feature
Tags: Life Insurance, Policy Feature, Permanent Insurance
Related Nodes: KN-001

payload.tags: Life Insurance, Policy Feature, Permanent Insurance

text:
Node 96 — Cash Value
Definition: A savings element within a permanent life insurance policy that the policyowner can access (via loans or surrender) while the insured is alive. Its value grows over time.
Category: Feature
Tags: Life Insurance, Savings, Policy Value
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-004, KN-005

text:
Node 97 — Policy Loan
Definition: An advance payment of a portion of the policy's value that the policyowner can borrow from the insurer, using the cash value as collateral. It reduces the policy's death benefit and cash value until repaid.
Category: Action/Feature
Tags: Life Insurance, Cash Value, Loan
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-003

text:
Node 98 — Policy Surrender
Definition: The act of terminating a cash value life insurance policy in exchange for its cash surrender value (the cash value minus any outstanding loans or charges).
Category: Action/Feature
Tags: Life Insurance, Cash Value, Policy Termination
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-003

text:
Node 99 — Traditional Whole Life Insurance
Definition: The most basic type of cash value life insurance, providing a constant amount of lifetime coverage with a level premium rate that does not increase with age.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Permanent Insurance
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-007, KN-008, KN-009

text:
Node 100 — Single-Premium Whole Life Insurance
Definition: A type of whole life insurance where the entire premium for lifetime coverage is paid in one lump sum upfront, creating a significant cash value immediately.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Premium Payment
Related Nodes: KN-006

text:
Node 101 — Limited-Payment Whole Life Insurance
Definition: A type of whole life insurance where premiums are paid for a specified period (e.g., 20 years or until age 65), after which the policy is "paid-up" and coverage continues for life with no further payments.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Premium Payment
Related Nodes: KN-006

payload.tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Premium Payment

text:
Node 102 — Continuous-Payment Whole Life Insurance
Definition: A type of whole life insurance where level premiums are payable for the insured's entire lifetime or until death. This option has the lowest periodic premium for a given face amount.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Premium Payment
Related Nodes: KN-006

text:
Node 103 — Modified-Premium Whole Life Insurance
Definition: A type of whole life insurance with a lower premium for an initial period (e.g., 10 years), which then increases to a higher, level premium for the remainder of the policy's life. The death benefit remains constant.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Modified Policy
Related Nodes: KN-006

payload.tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Modified Policy

text:
Node 104 — Modified Coverage Whole Life Insurance
Definition: A type of whole life insurance where the face amount of coverage changes at stated intervals (typically decreasing over time), while the premium remains level for the life of the policy.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Modified Policy
Related Nodes: KN-006

text:
Node 105 — Joint Whole Life Insurance
Definition: A whole life policy that insures two people and pays the death benefit upon the death of the first insured. Coverage then terminates.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Joint Policy
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-013

text:
Node 106 — Last Survivor Life Insurance
Definition: A whole life policy that insures two or more people and pays the death benefit only after the last insured person has died. It is often used for estate planning.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Whole Life, Joint Policy
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-012

text:
Node 107 — Universal Life (UL) Insurance
Definition: A flexible form of permanent life insurance characterized by flexible premiums, a flexible face amount/death benefit, and separate pricing factors (mortality, interest, expenses).
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Universal Life, Permanent Insurance
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-015, KN-016, KN-017

payload.tags: Life Insurance, Universal Life, Permanent Insurance

text:
Node 108 — Flexible Premiums
Definition: A key feature of universal life insurance allowing the policyowner to vary the amount and timing of premium payments, as long as the cash value is sufficient to cover policy charges.
Category: Feature
Tags: Universal Life, Premium Payment
Related Nodes: KN-014, KN-020

text:
Node 109 — Flexible Death Benefit
Definition: A feature of universal life insurance that allows the policyowner to choose between a level death benefit or a death benefit that increases along with the policy's cash value.
Category: Feature
Tags: Universal Life, Death Benefit
Related Nodes: KN-014, KN-020

text:
Node 110 — Indexed Universal Life (IUL) Insurance
Definition: A type of UL insurance where the cash value's interest crediting is tied to the performance of a stock market index (e.g., S&P 500), with a guaranteed minimum interest rate (floor) and a maximum rate (cap).
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Universal Life, Indexed Products
Related Nodes: KN-014

payload.tags: Life Insurance, Universal Life, Indexed Products

text:
Node 111 — Variable Life (VL) Insurance
Definition: A type of permanent life insurance with fixed premiums where the policyowner allocates the cash value into various investment subaccounts. The death benefit and cash value fluctuate with investment performance.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Variable Products, Securities
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-019, KN-020

text:
Node 112 — Subaccounts (Separate Account)
Definition: Investment portfolios within a variable insurance product, similar to mutual funds, where the policyowner directs premium payments. These are held in the insurer's separate account, segregated from its general assets.
Category: Concept
Tags: Variable Products, Investments, Securities
Related Nodes: KN-018, KN-020

text:
Node 113 — Variable Universal Life (VUL) Insurance
Definition: A product that combines the features of Variable Life (investment subaccounts) and Universal Life (flexible premiums and death benefits), giving the policyowner maximum control and investment risk.
Category: Product
Tags: Life Insurance, Variable Products, Universal Life, Securities
Related Nodes: KN-014, KN-015, KN-016, KN-018, KN-019

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Life Insurance, Variable Products, Universal Life, Securities

text:
Node 114 — Annuity
Definition: A financial product issued by an insurer that promises to make a series of payments to a named person or entity in exchange for a premium. It offers a way to accumulate, protect, and distribute assets, often as a stream of income.
Category: Annuity Basics
Tags: Financial Product, Insurance, Retirement, Income Stream
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-003, KN-006, KN-012
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Financial Product, Insurance, Retirement, Income Stream

text:
Node 115 — Key Players in an Annuity
Definition: The individuals or entities involved in an annuity contract. This includes the Contract Owner (purchaser), Annuitant (whose life measures payments), Payee (receives payments if not the annuitant), Beneficiary (receives death benefits), and Joint Annuitant.
Category: Annuity Basics
Tags: Contract Owner, Annuitant, Beneficiary, Payee
Related Nodes: KN-001
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 116 — Qualified vs. Nonqualified Annuity
Definition: A Qualified Annuity is purchased to fund or distribute funds from a retirement plan or IRA, receiving specific tax treatment. A Nonqualified Annuity is purchased outside of a retirement plan.
Category: Annuity Basics
Tags: Qualified Annuity, Nonqualified Annuity, Retirement Plan, IRA, Taxation
Related Nodes: KN-001
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Qualified Annuity, Nonqualified Annuity, Retirement Plan, IRA, Taxation

text:
Node 117 — Single-Premium Immediate Annuity (SPIA)
Definition: An annuity purchased with a single lump-sum premium that begins making payments within one year of purchase. It is used to convert a lump sum into an immediate income stream.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: Immediate Annuity, SPIA, Income Stream, Payout Period
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-007, KN-012
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Immediate Annuity, SPIA, Income Stream, Payout Period

text:
Node 118 — Deferred Annuity
Definition: An annuity with an accumulation period, where funds grow over time before payments begin. The contract owner can access funds via withdrawals, surrender, or annuitization. Payments start more than one year after purchase.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: Deferred Annuity, SPDA, FPDA, Accumulation Period
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-006, KN-016
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Deferred Annuity, SPDA, FPDA, Accumulation Period

text:
Node 119 — Accumulation Period
Definition: The phase of a deferred annuity from the purchase date until annuity payments begin or the contract is surrendered. During this time, the premium(s) and earnings grow, creating the accumulated value.
Category: Contract Features
Tags: Deferred Annuity, Accumulated Value, Savings
Related Nodes: KN-005
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 120 — Payout Options
Definition: The methods a contract owner can choose for receiving annuity payments. The choice affects the payment amount and duration. Options can be life-contingent (based on life expectancy) or non-life-contingent.
Category: Payout Options
Tags: Income Distribution, Life Annuity, Period Certain, Refund
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-008, KN-009, KN-010, KN-011
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Income Distribution, Life Annuity, Period Certain, Refund

text:
Node 121 — Life Annuity (Straight Life)
Definition: A payout option that provides payments for the lifetime of the annuitant. Payments cease upon the annuitant's death, regardless of the total amount paid out.
Category: Payout Options
Tags: Life Only, Straight Life, Lifetime Income
Related Nodes: KN-007
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Life Only, Straight Life, Lifetime Income

text:
Node 122 — Life Income with Period Certain
Definition: A payout option that guarantees payments for the annuitant's lifetime and for a minimum specified period (e.g., 10 or 20 years). If the annuitant dies before the period ends, a beneficiary receives the remaining payments.
Category: Payout Options
Tags: Period Certain, Guarantee Period, Beneficiary
Related Nodes: KN-007
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 123 — Joint and Survivor Annuity
Definition: A payout option based on the lives of two annuitants. Payments continue until the last annuitant dies. Payment amounts may or may not decrease after the first death.
Category: Payout Options
Tags: Joint Annuitant, Survivor Benefit, Lifetime Income
Related Nodes: KN-007, KN-002
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Joint Annuitant, Survivor Benefit, Lifetime Income

text:
Node 124 — Fixed Period / Fixed Amount Annuity
Definition: Non-life-contingent payout options. A Fixed Period Annuity guarantees payments for a specified time. A Fixed Amount Annuity provides payments of a specified amount until the funds are exhausted.
Category: Payout Options
Tags: Fixed Period, Fixed Amount, Non-Life Contingent
Related Nodes: KN-007
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Fixed Period, Fixed Amount, Non-Life Contingent

text:
Node 125 — Annuitization
Definition: The process of converting the accumulated value of a deferred annuity into a stream of periodic income payments. Once annuitized, the deferred annuity functions like an immediate annuity.
Category: Contract Features
Tags: Payout Period, Income Stream, Conversion
Related Nodes: KN-005, KN-004
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 126 — Surrender Charge
Definition: A fee imposed by the insurer if a contract owner withdraws excess funds or fully surrenders a deferred annuity before the end of the surrender charge period. It helps the insurer recoup acquisition costs.
Category: Contract Features
Tags: Withdrawal, Surrender, CDSC, Penalty
Related Nodes: KN-005
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 127 — Fixed Annuity
Definition: A type of annuity where the insurer bears the investment risk. It offers guaranteed principal and a minimum interest-crediting rate. Premiums are held in the insurer's general account.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: Fixed Annuity, Principal Guarantee, Investment Risk
Related Nodes: KN-015, KN-016
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Fixed Annuity, Principal Guarantee, Investment Risk

text:
Node 128 — Fixed-Rate Deferred Annuity (FRDA)
Definition: A deferred annuity that guarantees a minimum interest-crediting rate and may offer a higher current rate for a specified term. It protects principal and allows for tax-deferred growth.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: FRDA, Fixed Annuity, Deferred Annuity, Interest Rate
Related Nodes: KN-014, KN-005
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: FRDA, Fixed Annuity, Deferred Annuity, Interest Rate

text:
Node 129 — Fixed Indexed Annuity (FIA)
Definition: A fixed annuity that offers a guaranteed minimum interest rate plus the potential for additional earnings based on the performance of an external stock market index (reference index). Principal is protected from market loss.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: FIA, Indexed Annuity, S&P 500, Principal Guarantee
Related Nodes: KN-014, KN-005
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: FIA, Indexed Annuity, S&P 500, Principal Guarantee

text:
Node 130 — Variable Annuity (VA)
Definition: An annuity where the contract owner bears the investment risk by allocating premiums into investment options called subaccounts. The accumulated value and payments can fluctuate with market performance. It is registered as a security.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: Variable Annuity, Investment Risk, Subaccount, SEC
Related Nodes: KN-018, KN-019, KN-020, KN-021
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities

payload.tags: Variable Annuity, Investment Risk, Subaccount, SEC

text:
Node 131 — Subaccount
Definition: Investment portfolios within an insurer's separate account, available in variable annuities. Each subaccount has a distinct investment strategy and risk profile, similar to a mutual fund.
Category: Contract Features
Tags: Separate Account, Investment Choice, Variable Annuity
Related Nodes: KN-017
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: Separate Account, Investment Choice, Variable Annuity

text:
Node 132 — Guaranteed Minimum Death Benefit (GMDB)
Definition: A rider on a variable annuity that guarantees the beneficiary will receive at least a specified minimum amount upon the annuitant's death, regardless of the contract's market performance (e.g., return of premium).
Category: Contract Features
Tags: Rider, Death Benefit, Guarantee, Beneficiary
Related Nodes: KN-017
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 133 — Living Benefit Riders
Definition: Optional riders on deferred annuities (especially VAs) that provide protection from market downturns by guaranteeing certain income, withdrawal, or accumulation amounts (e.g., GMIB, GLWB, GMAB).
Category: Contract Features
Tags: Rider, Guarantee, Lifetime Income, Withdrawal
Related Nodes: KN-017, KN-021
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 134 — Registered Index-Linked Annuity (RILA)
Definition: A variable annuity that links potential returns to a market index. It offers some protection against investment loss through buffers or floors, but the owner can still lose principal. It is registered as a security.
Category: Annuity Types
Tags: RILA, Indexed Annuity, Buffer, Floor, Variable Annuity
Related Nodes: KN-017, KN-016, KN-022, KN-023
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition
payload.tags: RILA, Indexed Annuity, Buffer, Floor, Variable Annuity

text:
Node 135 — RILA Buffer
Definition: A feature of a RILA that protects the contract's accumulated value from an initial amount of index loss. The owner absorbs losses that exceed the specified buffer percentage (e.g., with a 10% buffer, a 15% loss results in a 5% loss to the owner).
Category: Contract Features
Tags: RILA, Downside Protection, Risk Management
Related Nodes: KN-021
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 136 — RILA Floor
Definition: A feature of a RILA that sets a maximum potential loss for the contract owner. The insurer absorbs any index losses beyond the specified floor percentage (e.g., with a 10% floor, the owner's maximum loss is capped at 10%).
Category: Contract Features
Tags: RILA, Downside Protection, Risk Management
Related Nodes: KN-021
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 3 - Annuities
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 3 - Annuities | LOMA281_M2L3_Knowledge File_Annuities

text:
Node 137 — Health Insurance
Definition: A type of insurance that protects against the risk of financial loss from illness or injury. It comes in three main forms: medical expense, long-term care, and disability income coverage.
Category: Insurance Type
Tags: insurance, health, medical
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-003, KN-004

text:
Node 138 — Medical Expense Coverage
Definition: A form of health insurance that provides benefits to pay for the treatment of illnesses and injuries, including hospital, surgical, and physician expenses.
Category: Insurance Type
Tags: health insurance, medical, coverage
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-005, KN-008

text:
Node 139 — Long-Term Care (LTC) Coverage
Definition: A form of health insurance that provides benefits for medical and other services to insureds who need constant care for an extended period at home or in a qualified facility.
Category: Insurance Type
Tags: health insurance, LTC, extended care
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-018, KN-019

text:
Node 140 — Disability Income Coverage
Definition: A form of health insurance that provides income replacement benefits to an insured who is unable to work because of illness or injury.
Category: Insurance Type
Tags: health insurance, disability, income replacement
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-022, KN-024

text:
Node 141 — Major Medical Coverage
Definition: The most common type of medical expense insurance, which pays benefits for a wide range of expenses including hospital, surgical, physician, prescription drugs, and some preventive care.
Category: Insurance Product
Tags: medical expense, comprehensive coverage

text:
Node 142 — Cost Participation Methods
Definition: Features in medical expense policies that require the insured to share in the costs of medical care. Common methods include deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: cost sharing, out-of-pocket

text:
Node 143 — Deductible
Definition: A flat dollar amount of eligible medical expenses that the insured must pay before the insurance policy begins to pay benefits.
Category: Cost Participation Method
Tags: cost sharing, out-of-pocket, deductible
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-008, KN-009

text:
Node 144 — Coinsurance
Definition: A percentage of the allowable medical expenses (after the deductible is met) that the insured must pay.
Category: Cost Participation Method
Tags: cost sharing, out-of-pocket, percentage
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-007, KN-009

text:
Node 145 — Maximum Out-of-Pocket Provision
Definition: A policy feature, also known as a stop-loss provision, that limits the total amount an insured must pay for deductibles and coinsurance in a year. Once the limit is reached, the insurer pays 100% of covered expenses.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: cost sharing, stop-loss, out-of-pocket limit

Node 146 — Affordable Care Act (ACA)

Node 147 — Supplemental Medical Expense Coverage
Definition: Optional policies offered for an additional fee to cover services typically excluded from major medical insurance, such as dental, vision, or dread disease.

Node 148 — Managed Care Plan
Definition: A type of health insurance plan that aims to manage medical care access and costs by using provider networks, primary care providers (PCPs), and emphasizing preventive care.

text:
Node 149 — Primary Care Provider (PCP)
Definition: A physician in a managed care plan who coordinates an insured's medical care and provides referrals to specialists.
Category: Role
Tags: managed care, gatekeeper, physician
Related Nodes: KN-012, KN-015, KN-017
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 150 — Copayment
Definition: A specified, fixed dollar amount that a member of a managed care plan must pay a network provider each time they receive a service.
Category: Cost Participation Method
Tags: cost sharing, fixed fee, copay
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-012
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 151 — Health Maintenance Organization (HMO)
Definition: A type of managed care plan that requires members to use in-network providers and obtain referrals from a PCP to see specialists. Out-of-network care is typically not covered.
Category: Managed Care Plan
Tags: HMO, in-network, PCP
Related Nodes: KN-012, KN-016, KN-017
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 152 — Preferred Provider Organization (PPO)
Definition: A type of managed care plan that offers more flexibility than an HMO. It does not require a PCP or referrals and provides benefits for both in-network (at a higher level) and out-of-network providers (at a lower level).
Category: Managed Care Plan
Tags: PPO, in-network, out-of-network
Related Nodes: KN-012, KN-015, KN-017
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 153 — Point of Service (POS) Plan
Definition: A hybrid managed care plan that combines features of HMOs and PPOs. It usually requires a PCP and referrals but allows members to seek care out-of-network at a higher cost.
Category: Managed Care Plan
Tags: POS, hybrid, PCP
Related Nodes: KN-012, KN-015, KN-016
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 154 — Consumer-Driven Health Plan (CDHP)
Definition: A health plan that combines a high-deductible health plan (HDHP) with a tax-advantaged savings account (like an HSA or HRA) that is used to pay for routine medical expenses.
Category: Health Plan Type
Tags: CDHP, HDHP, HSA, HRA
Related Nodes: KN-001
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 155 — Long-Term Care (LTC) Benefit Triggers
Definition: Conditions specified in an LTC policy that establish an insured's eligibility for benefits. The two most common triggers are the inability to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) and cognitive impairment.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: LTC, benefit trigger, eligibility
Related Nodes: KN-003, KN-020, KN-021
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 156 — Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
Definition: Basic self-care tasks used as a measure of functional status. They include eating, bathing, dressing, continence, toileting, and transferring. An inability to perform a specified number of these can trigger LTC benefits.
Category: Benefit Trigger
Tags: LTC, ADL, functional impairment
Related Nodes: KN-019
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 157 — Cognitive Impairment
Definition: A reduction in a person's ability to think, reason, or remember (e.g., Alzheimer's disease, dementia). If severe enough to require supervision, it can trigger LTC benefits, regardless of ADL capability.
Category: Benefit Trigger
Tags: LTC, cognitive impairment, dementia
Related Nodes: KN-019
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 158 — Total Disability
Definition: A condition defined in a disability income policy to determine eligibility for benefits. Common definitions include "current usual," "own previous occupation," and "income loss."
Category: Disability Definition
Tags: disability, eligibility, definition
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-023
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 159 — Presumptive Disability
Definition: A condition (e.g., total blindness, loss of two limbs) that, if present, automatically qualifies the insured as totally disabled under a disability income policy, allowing them to receive full benefits even if they return to work.
Category: Disability Definition
Tags: disability, automatic qualification
Related Nodes: KN-022
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 160 — Elimination Period
Definition: The waiting period in a disability income policy; it is the length of time an insured must be disabled before benefit payments begin.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: disability, waiting period
Related Nodes: KN-004
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 161 — Social Security Disability Income (SSDI)
Definition: A U.S. federal program that provides disability income payments to workers under age 65 who have paid sufficient Social Security taxes and meet a strict definition of total disability.
Category: Government Program
Tags: disability, social security, SSDI
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-026, KN-027
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 162 — Supplemental Security Income (SSI)
Definition: A U.S. federal program providing disability income benefits to people with limited income and resources who are disabled, blind, or age 65 or older. It is based on financial need, not work history.
Category: Government Program
Tags: disability, needs-based, SSI
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-025
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson: Lesson 4 - Health Insurance
Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Node 163 — Workers' Compensation
Definition: A state-mandated program that provides medical expense and wage replacement benefits to workers who are disabled as a result of a work-related accident or illness.
Category: Government Program
Tags: disability, work-related, state program
Related Nodes: KN-004, KN-025

Path: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module 2 - Individual Insurance Products | Lesson 4 - Health Insurance | LOMA281_M2L4_Knowledge File_Health Insurance

text:
Node 164 — Supplemental Benefit (Rider)
Definition: An optional benefit that can be added to a basic life insurance policy for an additional premium. Riders expand coverage to meet specific financial needs.
Category: General Concept
Tags: rider, add-on, supplemental coverage
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-006, KN-009, KN-013

text:
Node 165 — Disability Benefits Rider
Definition: A category of riders that provide protection if the insured or policyowner becomes totally disabled.
Category: Rider Category
Tags: disability, waiver of premium, income replacement
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-003, KN-004, KN-005
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 3 - Benefits, Provisions, and Ownership Rights | Lesson: Lesson 1 - Supplemental Benefits

text:
Node 166 — Waiver of Premium for Disability (WP)
Definition: A rider that waives the policy's premiums if the insured becomes totally disabled after a waiting period, keeping the policy in force.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: disability, waiver of premium, insured
Related Nodes: KN-002

text:
Node 167 — Waiver of Premium for Payor
Definition: A rider for third-party policies (e.g., parent owning a policy on a child) that waives premiums if the policyowner (payor) dies or becomes totally disabled.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: disability, waiver of premium, payor, third-party
Related Nodes: KN-002

text:
Node 168 — Disability Income (DI) Benefit Rider
Definition: A rider that pays a specified monthly income to the policyowner if the insured becomes totally disabled.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: disability, income replacement, monthly benefit
Related Nodes: KN-002

text:
Node 169 — Accidental Death Benefits Rider
Definition: A category of riders that pay an additional benefit if the insured's death is the result of an accident.
Category: Rider Category
Tags: accident, death benefit, AD&D
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-007, KN-008
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 3 - Benefits, Provisions, and Ownership Rights | Lesson: Lesson 1 - Supplemental Benefits

text:
Node 170 — Accidental Death Benefit (ADB)
Definition: A rider that pays an additional sum, often equal to the policy's face amount (known as "double indemnity"), if the insured dies as a direct result of an accident.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: accident, double indemnity, ADB
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-008
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 3 - Benefits, Provisions, and Ownership Rights | Lesson: Lesson 1 - Supplemental Benefits

text:
Node 171 — Accidental Death and Dismemberment (AD&D)
Definition: A rider that pays a benefit for either accidental death or for the accidental loss of two limbs or the sight in both eyes. It does not pay for both events from the same accident.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: accident, dismemberment, AD&D
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-007
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 3 - Benefits, Provisions, and Ownership Rights | Lesson: Lesson 1 - Supplemental Benefits

text:
Node 172 — Accelerated Death Benefit (Living Benefit)
Definition: A category of riders that allow the policyowner to receive a portion of the policy's death benefit while the insured is still alive, under specific circumstances like a terminal illness.
Category: Rider Category
Tags: living benefit, accelerated benefit, early payout
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-010, KN-011, KN-012
Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 3 - Benefits, Provisions, and Ownership Rights | Lesson: Lesson 1 - Supplemental Benefits

payload.tags: living benefit, accelerated benefit, early payout

text:
Node 173 — Terminal Illness (TI) Benefit Rider
Definition: An accelerated benefit rider that pays a portion of the death benefit if the insured is diagnosed with a terminal illness and has a short life expectancy (e.g., 12-24 months).
Category: Rider Type
Tags: living benefit, terminal illness
Related Nodes: KN-009

text:
Node 174 — Dread Disease (DD) Benefit Rider
Definition: An accelerated benefit rider that pays a portion of the death benefit if the insured is diagnosed with a specific critical illness listed in the policy, such as cancer or stroke.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: living benefit, critical illness, dread disease
Related Nodes: KN-009

text:
Node 175 — Long-Term Care (LTC) Insurance Rider
Definition: An accelerated benefit rider that pays a monthly benefit if the insured requires long-term care services. The total benefit paid is deducted from the policy's death benefit.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: living benefit, LTC, long-term care
Related Nodes: KN-009

text:
Node 176 — Riders to Cover Additional People
Definition: A category of riders that add term life insurance coverage for other family members (e.g., spouse, children) to the primary insured's policy.
Category: Rider Category
Tags: family coverage, term rider
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-014, KN-015

text:
Node 177 — Spouse and Children’s Insurance Rider
Definition: A rider that provides term life insurance for the insured's spouse and all of their children, often sold in "coverage units" for a flat premium rate for the children.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: family coverage, term rider, child rider
Related Nodes: KN-013

text:
Node 178 — Second Insured Rider
Definition: A rider that provides term life insurance for another person, such as a spouse or business partner, with the premium based on that person's individual risk factors.
Category: Rider Type
Tags: term rider, joint life
Related Nodes: KN-013

text:
Node 179 — Standard Provisions
Definition: Clauses mandated by state law that must be included in every life insurance policy to protect the policyowner and define the rights and obligations of both the insurer and the policyowner.
Category: Policy Structure
Tags: regulation, policy clause, consumer protection
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-003, KN-004, KN-005, KN-006

text:
Node 180 — Entire Contract Provision
Definition: A standard provision stating that the life insurance policy, along with the attached application, constitutes the complete and entire agreement between the policyowner and the insurer.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: policy contract, application, legal agreement
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 181 — Insuring Clause
Definition: A standard provision that represents the insurer's fundamental promise to pay the specified death benefit to the beneficiary upon the insured's death, provided all conditions are met.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: promise to pay, death benefit, insurer obligation
Related Nodes: KN-001

payload.tags: promise to pay, death benefit, insurer obligation

text:
Node 182 — Free Look Provision
Definition: A standard provision that grants the policyowner a specific period (e.g., 10 days) after policy delivery to review the contract and return it for a full refund of all premiums paid.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: right to return, refund, trial period
Related Nodes: KN-001

payload.tags: right to return, refund, trial period

text:
Node 183 — Consideration Clause
Definition: A standard provision that specifies what each party gives in the contract: the policyowner's consideration is the premium payments, and the insurer's consideration is the promise to pay the death benefit.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: premium, promise to pay, exchange of value
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 184 — Owner's Rights
Definition: A standard provision outlining the policyowner's privileges, which include naming and changing beneficiaries, accessing the cash value through loans or surrender, and assigning the policy.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: policy ownership, control, privileges
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-007, KN-012, KN-014

text:
Node 185 — Beneficiary Designation
Definition: The act of naming the person(s) or entity who will receive the policy's death benefit. Designations can be primary, contingent, revocable, or irrevocable.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: beneficiary, death benefit, designation
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-008

text:
Node 186 — Revocable vs. Irrevocable Beneficiary
Definition: A Revocable Beneficiary can be changed by the policyowner at any time. An Irrevocable Beneficiary has a vested interest in the policy, and their designation cannot be changed without their written consent.
Category: Beneficiary Type
Tags: beneficiary, revocable, irrevocable, vested rights

text:
Node 187 — Grace Period Provision
Definition: A standard provision that allows the policy to remain in force for a set period (usually 31 days) after a premium due date has passed. If the insured dies during this period, the overdue premium is deducted from the death benefit.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: late payment, lapse prevention, grace period
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-010

payload.tags: late payment, lapse prevention, grace period

text:
Node 188 — Automatic Premium Loan (APL)
Definition: An optional provision in cash value policies that automatically pays a past-due premium by taking a loan from the policy's cash value, preventing the policy from lapsing.
Category: Optional Provision
Tags: lapse prevention, policy loan, APL
Related Nodes: KN-009, KN-011, KN-012

text:
Node 189 — Reinstatement Provision
Definition: A standard provision that allows the owner of a lapsed policy to put it back in force by paying all back premiums plus interest and providing proof of insurability.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: lapse, reactivation, proof of insurability
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-010

text:
Node 190 — Policy Loan Provision
Definition: A standard provision in cash value policies that allows the policyowner to borrow money from the policy's accumulated cash value. Unpaid loans reduce the death benefit.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: cash value, loan, policy debt
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-010

text:
Node 191 — Incontestability Clause
Definition: A standard provision that prevents the insurer from contesting the validity of the policy (e.g., due to misstatements on the application) after the policy has been in force for a specified period, typically two years.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: contestable period, misrepresentation, policy validity
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 192 — Assignment Provision
Definition: A standard provision outlining the policyowner's right to transfer their ownership rights to another party (an assignee). The two types are absolute and collateral assignment.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: transfer of ownership, assignment, collateral
Related Nodes: KN-006, KN-015, KN-016

text:
Node 193 — Absolute Assignment
Definition: The permanent and complete transfer of all ownership rights in a policy from the current policyowner to an assignee.
Category: Assignment Type
Tags: transfer of ownership, absolute assignment
Related Nodes: KN-014

text:
Node 194 — Collateral Assignment
Definition: The temporary transfer of some policy rights to a creditor as security for a debt. The assignment terminates when the debt is repaid.
Category: Assignment Type
Tags: loan security, collateral, temporary transfer
Related Nodes: KN-014

text:
Node 195 — Suicide Clause
Definition: A standard provision stating that if the insured commits suicide within a specific period from the policy's issue date (usually two years), the insurer will not pay the death benefit but will return the premiums paid.
Category: Standard Provision
Tags: suicide, exclusion, return of premium
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-018

text:
Node 196 — Standard Exclusions
Definition: Conditions or circumstances stipulated in the policy for which the death benefit will not be paid. Common exclusions include war, aviation, hazardous occupations, and commission of a felony.
Category: Policy Structure
Tags: exclusion, limitation, uncovered risk
Related Nodes: KN-001, KN-017

text:
Node 197 — Nonforfeiture Options
Definition: Contractual rights available to a cash value policyowner who stops paying premiums. They guarantee that the owner will not forfeit (lose) the policy's accumulated cash value.
Category: Policy Provision
Tags: nonforfeiture, cash value, policy lapse, consumer protection
Related Nodes: KN-002, KN-003, KN-004, KN-005

text:
Node 198 — Standard Nonforfeiture Law
Definition: A state-level consumer protection law requiring that cash value life insurance policies must include provisions specifying how the cash value will be made available to the owner if the policy lapses.
Category: Regulation
Tags: nonforfeiture, state law, consumer protection
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 199 — Cash Surrender Option
Definition: A nonforfeiture option where the policyowner terminates the policy in exchange for its cash surrender value (the accumulated cash value minus any outstanding loans).
Category: Nonforfeiture Option
Tags: cash value, surrender, policy termination
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 200 — Reduced Paid-Up Insurance Option
Definition: A nonforfeiture option where the policyowner uses the net cash value as a single premium to purchase a new, fully paid-up whole life policy. The new policy has a lower face amount but provides lifetime coverage.
Category: Nonforfeiture Option
Tags: reduced paid-up, single premium, lifetime coverage
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 201 — Extended Term Insurance Option
Definition: A nonforfeiture option where the policyowner uses the net cash value as a single premium to purchase a term life insurance policy. The term policy has the same face amount as the original policy and lasts for as long a period as the cash value can purchase. This is typically the default option.
Category: Nonforfeiture Option
Tags: extended term, term insurance, default option
Related Nodes: KN-001

Node 202 — Policy Dividends
Definition: A partial refund of premiums paid on a participating life insurance policy, issued by a mutual insurer. Dividends reflect the insurer's better-than-expected results in mortality, investment returns, and expenses. They are not guaranteed.
Category: Policy Feature
Tags: dividend, participating policy, mutual insurer, return of premium

Node 203 — Cash Dividend Option
Definition: A dividend option where the insurer sends the policyowner a check for the amount of the annual dividend.
Category: Dividend Option

Node 204 — Apply to Premiums Dividend Option
Definition: A dividend option where the policyowner directs the insurer to use the annual dividend to pay a portion of the next premium due, thereby reducing the owner's out-of-pocket cost.
Category: Dividend Option
Tags: premium reduction, dividend

Node 205 — Accumulate at Interest Dividend Option
Definition: A dividend option where the policyowner leaves the dividends with the insurer to grow in a separate savings account that earns a specified rate of interest. The dividends remain withdrawable.

Node 206 — Paid-Up Additions (PUA) Dividend Option
Definition: A dividend option where the policyowner uses the annual dividend as a single premium to purchase small, additional amounts of fully paid-up whole life insurance. These additions increase the policy's death benefit and cash value.

Tags: paid-up additions, PUA, single premium, dividend

Node 207 — One-Year Term Insurance Dividend Option
Definition: A dividend option where the policyowner uses the annual dividend to purchase one-year term life insurance, providing a temporary increase to the policy's death benefit.

text:
Node 208 — Group Insurance
Definition: A type of insurance that insures the members of a specific group, such as the employees of a company, under a single contract. It contrasts with individual insurance, which covers one person or family.
Category: Insurance Type
Tags: group coverage, employee benefits

text:
Node 209 — Master Group Insurance Contract
Definition: The single insurance contract issued to the sponsor of a group (e.g., an employer) that establishes the group insurance plan and provides coverage for all eligible members of the group.
Category: Group Insurance Contract

Node 211 — Certificate of Insurance
Definition: A document provided to each insured member of a group plan. Its purpose is to describe the benefits, rights, and coverage provided under the master group insurance contract.

text:
Node 212 — Contributory vs. Noncontributory Plans
Definition: A key distinction in group insurance plans based on who pays the premium. In contributory plans, members contribute to the premium cost. In noncontributory plans, the group sponsor pays the entire premium.
Category: Plan Administration
Tags: premium payment, contributory, noncontributory
Related Nodes: KN-001

text:
Node 213 — Insurer-Administered vs. Self-Administered Plans
Definition: A distinction based on how a group insurance plan is managed. In an insurer-administered plan, the insurer handles administrative tasks. In a self-administered plan, the group sponsor manages tasks like enrollment and premium collection.
Category: Plan Administration
Tags: plan management, administration
Related Nodes: KN-001

Node 214 — Group Underwriting
Definition: The process of assessing the risk of an entire group to determine the terms of coverage and premiums. The learning objectives state that the factors used in this process are a key topic.

Node 219 — Benefit Schedules
Definition: A provision in a group life policy that specifies the amount of insurance coverage each eligible member receives, often based on a formula related to salary, position, or a flat amount for all members.

Node 220 — Coverage Termination
Definition: The cessation of a group member's life insurance coverage, which can occur if the group policy is terminated or if the member no longer meets the eligibility requirements (e.g., leaves their job).

Node 221 — Conversion Right (Group Life)
Definition: The contractual right of a group member whose coverage terminates to convert their group coverage into an individual life insurance policy without providing proof of insurability.

Tags: conversion privilege, portability, individual policy

Node 225 — Group Creditor Life Insurance

Node 228 — Defined Benefit (DB) Plan
Definition: A type of qualified retirement plan where the employer promises a specific, pre-determined retirement benefit to the employee, typically based on a formula using salary and years of service. The employer bears the investment risk.

Tags: DB plan, pension, defined benefit

Category: Core Concept
Tags: risk-return, investment strategy, tradeoff

Definition: The end-to-end sequence of activities for handling a claim, typically including receiving notice of loss, obtaining proof of loss, evaluating the claim's validity, calculating the benefit, and paying the benefit or denying the claim.

text:
Lesson 1 - Risky Business (Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition, Module 1 - Risk and Insurance)
Number of nodes: 28
Concepts covered:
- Risk: The possibility of loss or an undesirable outcome.
- Speculative Risk: Risk with the potential for gain or loss. Not insurable.
- Pure Risk: Risk with only a chance of loss or no loss. Insurable.
- Longevity Risk: The risk of outliving one's financial resources.
- Risk Management: The process of identifying, assessing, and handling risk.
- Risk Avoidance: Eliminating the chance of loss by avoiding the activity.
- Risk Control: Reducing the frequency or severity of loss.
- Risk Transfer: Shifting risk to another party, typically via insurance.
- Risk Acceptance: Retaining the risk and bearing potential loss.
- Insurance: A contract transferring risk from an individual to an insurer in exchange for a premium.
- Risk Pooling: Sharing risk among a group to minimize financial impact on any one member.
- Life Insurance: Insurance paying a benefit upon the death of the insured.
- Annuity: A contract providing periodic payments for life or a set period.
- Applicant: The person applying for an insurance policy.
- Policyowner: The person who owns and controls the insurance policy.
- Insured: The person whose life or health is covered by the policy.
- Beneficiary: The person who receives the insurance benefit.
- Third-Party Policy: A policy where the policyowner and the insured are different people.
- Insurable Risk: A risk meeting criteria (pure, definite, measurable, significant, non-catastrophic, predictable)
- Contract of Indemnity: Insurance paying for actual financial loss (not more, not less).
- Valued Contract: Insurance paying a fixed benefit amount agreed at policy issue.
- Loss Rate: The ratio of losses to the number of exposures or policies.
- Law of Large Numbers: Principle that predictions are more accurate with a larger group.
- Reinsurance: Insurance for insurers; transferring risk between insurance companies.
- Retention Limit: The maximum amount an insurer will keep before transferring risk.
- Direct Writer: The primary insurer issuing the policy to the customer.
- Reinsurer: The insurer accepting risk from another insurer.
- Retrocessionaire: A reinsurer who accepts risk from another reinsurer.

Lesson Summary (from source): LOMA 281 Module 2 Lesson 1 Term Life Insurance Summary of Key Points Term life insurance provides coverage for a specified period of time, called the policy term. The policy benefit will be payable ONLY if • The insured dies during the specified policy term, and • The policy is in force when the insured dies. Four types of term life insurance: • Level term life insurance─The policy benefit—and usually the premium—remains the same over the term. • Decreasing term life insurance─The policy benefit decreases over the policy term, but the premium amount usually remains the same. Examples are mortgage insurance, credit life insurance, and family income coverage. • Increasing term life insurance─The policy benefit increases at stated intervals over the policy term. Premiums increase as the benefit increases. • Return of premium (ROP) term life insurance─The policy pays a death benefit if the insured dies within the policy term or a return of premiums if the insured lives until the end of the policy term. Two options to continue life insurance coverage at end of term: • Policy renewal o Extends existing coverage for another term, usually the same length as original term, but sometimes shorter o Renewed coverage is usually for same face amount, but it can be for a lesser amount o Premiums for renewed coverage are based on the insured’s attained age at time of renewal o Subject to maximum number of renewals or maximum age limit o Does not require evidence of insurability • Policy Conversion o Allows policyowner to convert term policy to permanent (cash value) coverage during a specified time, known as the conversion period. o Does not require evidence of insurability o Converted coverage amount can be the same as or less than the original policy’s face amount o Premiums for converted coverage can be based on the insured’s attained age or original age Copyright © 2020 LOMA (Life Office Management Association, Inc.). All rights reserved.

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Lesson 2 - Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies (Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition, Module 1 - Risk and Insurance)
Number of nodes: 11
Concepts covered:
- Financial Services Industry Structure: The financial services industry is composed of banks, insurance companies, and securities firms, with trends toward convergence, consolidation, and globalization driven by deregulation.
- Types of Financial Institutions: Financial institutions include depository institutions (banks, credit unions), securities firms (including mutual funds), finance companies, and insurance companies.
- Types of Insurance Risks: Insurance covers personal risks, property damage, and liability, with life and health insurers covering personal risks, and property/casualty insurers covering property and liability.
- Insurance Company Organization Types: Insurance companies are usually organized as corporations for stability, and may be structured as stock insurers, mutual insurers, or fraternal benefit societies.
- Goals of Insurance Regulation: Insurance regulation aims to ensure insurer solvency and fair market conduct, focusing on financial stability and ethical business practices.
- Regulatory Agencies and Oversight: Insurance is regulated by national or state/provincial agencies, with the U.S. relying on state governments, federal oversight (FIO), and frameworks like McCarran-Ferguson and Dodd-Frank.
- NAIC and Regulatory Uniformity: The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) promotes regulatory uniformity among U.S. states for consistent insurance oversight and consumer protection.
- Licensing and Regulatory Requirements: Insurance companies must obtain licenses in each operating state, file policy forms, and undergo solvency and market conduct examinations to maintain compliance.
- Regulation of Variable Products: Variable life insurance and annuities are regulated by the SEC and require both state and FINRA licenses for sellers, reflecting their hybrid securities-insurance nature.
- Federal Laws Affecting Insurance: Federal laws, even if not directly targeted at insurance, impact the industry in areas like consumer protection, privacy, income tax, antiterrorism, and corporate governance.
- State Guaranty Associations: State guaranty associations protect policyholders if an insurer becomes insolvent, covering benefits typically up to $300,000.

Lesson Summary (from source): LOMA 281 Module 1 Lesson 2 Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies Summary of Key Points The financial services industry includes financial institutions that help people save, borrow, invest, and manage money. (Examples: banks, insurance companies, brokerage firms, mutual fund companies, and finance companies) Deregulation and other factors have contributed to the removal of barriers that historically prevented different financial institutions from competing with each other. This evolution of the insurance industry is characterized by • Convergence • Consolidation • Globalization Insurance companies sell products that help people manage • Personal risk • Property damage risk • Liability risk All life insurance companies are corporations that are distinct from their owners. Typically, insurance companies are organized as stock insurance companies, mutual insurance companies, or fraternal benefit societies. The primary goals of insurance regulation are to make sure insurers • Remain solvent—able to pay all obligations on time • Conduct their business fairly, honestly, and competently In the United States, insurance is regulated mainly by state insurance departments. States oversee insurers’ • Market conduct practices • Financial condition • Policy forms The federal government regulates • Sales of variable life insurance and annuities • Consumer protections • Consumer privacy • Income taxes • Antiterrorism Federal laws pertaining to insurance industry: McCarran-Ferguson Act and Dodd-Frank Act, which established the Federal Insurance Office (FIO). Copyright © 2020 LOMA (Life Office Management Association, Inc.). All rights reserved.

payload.course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition

text:
Lesson 3 - Life Insurance Policies as Contracts (Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition, Module 1 - Risk and Insurance)
Number of nodes: 23
Concepts covered:
